topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notification, crisp and white on the screen, felt like a physical blow. Liam stared at the email, his thumb hovering over the 'delete' button before snatching it back. The scholarship. The one thing keeping his entire future tethered to this university. He reread the fine print, the carefully worded clauses, searching for a loophole, a misplaced comma that would invalidate the entire thing. Every shadow in his cramped dorm room seemed to shift, coalescing into shapes that watched him from the periphery. He checked the windows, the door lock twice, the sound of the hallway traffic outside sounding amplified, menacing. Someone knew. They had orchestrated this. He started rearranging his books, stacking them into impossible towers, a futile attempt to impose order on the chaos churning in his gut. He kept glancing at his reflection in the darkened monitor, wondering if the expression on his face betrayed the sheer terror gripping him.

[story 2]

"It can't be," Maya whispered, her voice tight enough to snap. She traced the line item on the financial aid form again, her finger trembling slightly above the words. The discrepancy was glaring, undeniable. The funds allocated for her tuition, the exact amount she had worked so tirelessly to secure, were listed under a different name. A name that wasn't hers. A sudden chill swept through the room, despite the summer heat pressing against the windowpane. Maya began pacing, the rhythm jerky and uneven, her eyes darting towards the closed door of her room, then sweeping across the empty common area as if expecting an unseen observer to materialize. She gripped the edge of her desk until her knuckles were white, focusing intensely on the grain of the wood, trying to anchor herself against the dizzying sense of being observed, of being fundamentally misplaced in this space.

[story 3]

He kept checking the delivery notifications, refreshing the portal every thirty seconds. The confirmation was there, stark and unforgiving: the award was contingent on a specific criteria that, upon closer inspection, seemed to have been fulfilled by another applicant. A cold knot tightened in his stomach, spreading outward like spilled ink. Daniel didn't look up from his textbook, but Liam felt the weight of his gaze pressing down on him. He started talking to himself, muttering reassurances to the empty room, his voice low and strained. He moved from the desk to the window, pulling the curtain aside just an inch, peering out at the quad, convinced that every passing student was wearing an expression of knowing complicity. The silence of the apartment suddenly felt heavy, suffocating, as if the air itself was holding its breath, waiting for him to make the wrong move.
